title: Negative Sentence Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Making a sentence negative in English usually requires the helping verb 'do/does/did' plus 'not', or the negative form of 'be' or a modal verb - simply adding 'not' anywhere doesn't work the way it might in other languages. Here are 50 common negative sentence mistakes, explained simply, with the correct structure to use.

commonMistakes.right: I don't like this movie.
commonMistakes.why: In present simple, negatives need the helping verb 'do' plus 'not' (don't), not just 'not' placed directly before the verb. Always build negatives with: subject + don't/doesn't + base verb.

commonMistakes.right: She doesn't like coffee.
commonMistakes.why: With he/she/it, the negative uses 'doesn't' (not 'don't'), and the main verb returns to its base form without the 's'. Remember: doesn't already carries the 's', so the main verb stays plain.

commonMistakes.right: I don't want to go.
commonMistakes.why: 'No' is used before nouns to show absence (no money, no time), but it can't be used to negate a verb directly - verbs need 'don't/doesn't/didn't' instead. Keep 'no' for nouns, and 'don't/doesn't' for verbs.

commonMistakes.right: He doesn't go to school.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, 'no' cannot negate a verb - use 'doesn't' plus the base verb form instead. This mistake is very common among speakers whose first language uses a simple negative word before any part of speech.

commonMistakes.right: I didn't go there.
commonMistakes.why: Once 'didn't' is used, the main verb must return to its base form ('go'), not stay in the past tense ('went'). Remember: didn't already shows the past tense, so the main verb doesn't need to show it again.

commonMistakes.right: She didn't want to come.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, 'didn't' requires the base form of the verb afterward, not the past tense form. This is one of the most frequent negative sentence mistakes, worth practicing repeatedly until automatic.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not knowing the answer.
commonMistakes.right: I don't know the answer.
commonMistakes.why: This combines two separate mistakes: 'know' is a state verb that shouldn't be in continuous form, and the negative for present simple state verbs uses 'don't', not 'am not' plus '-ing'. Always negate state verbs with don't/doesn't, never am/is/are + not + -ing.

commonMistakes.right: I don't have any money. / I have no money.
commonMistakes.why: While 'have not' can work in very formal British English, the standard modern form uses 'don't have' or simply 'have no'. For everyday writing, choose 'don't have any' or 'have no' rather than 'have not any'.

commonMistakes.right: I can't swim. / I cannot swim.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has two errors: 'to' should never follow a modal verb like 'can', and 'can not' is usually written as one word, 'cannot' (or contracted as 'can't'). Remember: modal + not + base verb, with no 'to' in between.

commonMistakes.right: I can't help you.
commonMistakes.why: Modal verbs like 'can' form their own negative directly (can't), and should never be combined with 'don't'. Whenever a sentence has a modal verb, negate the modal itself, not with an extra 'don't'.

commonMistakes.right: She doesn't have time today.
commonMistakes.why: With he/she/it, you need 'doesn't', not 'don't' - and the main verb 'has' must return to its base form 'have'. Double-check both the helping verb agreement and the base form of the main verb together.

commonMistakes.right: They don't like the plan.
commonMistakes.why: With 'they' (plural), you need 'don't', not 'doesn't', which is reserved for he/she/it only. Match the negative helping verb to the subject just as carefully as you would in a positive sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not have a car.
commonMistakes.right: I don't have a car.
commonMistakes.why: Possession with 'have' uses 'don't/doesn't' for negation, not 'am/is/are not'. Keep this straight: 'have' meaning possession follows the do/does pattern for negatives, not the be-verb pattern.

commonMistakes.right: It isn't working well. / It doesn't work well.
commonMistakes.why: This has two issues: use 'well' (adverb) instead of 'good' (adjective) to describe how something works, and check whether you mean a temporary state ('isn't working' - broken right now) or a general truth ('doesn't work' - never functions properly). Choose your tense and word form carefully based on the intended meaning.

commonMistakes.right: I didn't see him yesterday.
commonMistakes.why: This mixes up two different tenses incorrectly - for a simple, one-time past action, use 'didn't' plus the base verb, not 'wasn't' plus the past participle (which would be used for other structures like passive voice). Use didn't + base verb for straightforward negative statements about the past.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not agree with this.
commonMistakes.right: I don't agree with this. / I disagree with this.
commonMistakes.why: 'Agree' is a regular action verb, not something used with 'am/is/are' - negate it with 'don't/doesn't' instead. Alternatively, you can use the opposite verb 'disagree' for a cleaner, more direct statement.

commonMistakes.wrong: I not have seen this movie.
commonMistakes.right: I haven't seen this movie.
commonMistakes.why: This present perfect negative needs 'haven't' (have + not, contracted), not 'not have' in that order. Keep the correct order: subject + haven't/hasn't + past participle.

commonMistakes.right: She hasn't seen that show.
commonMistakes.why: After 'hasn't', you need the past participle form of the verb ('seen'), not the simple past form ('saw'). This follows the same pattern as positive present perfect: have/has + past participle, whether positive or negative.

commonMistakes.wrong: I don't have never been there.
commonMistakes.right: I have never been there.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence combines two negative-related words incorrectly - 'don't have' and 'never' shouldn't be combined this way; simply use 'have never' with present perfect. Remove 'don't' entirely when using 'never' with present perfect tense.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not sure not about this.
commonMistakes.right: I am not sure about this.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has an accidental double 'not', which is simply a proofreading slip - remove the extra negative word. Always double-check your negative sentences for accidentally repeated negative words.

commonMistakes.right: He is never late. / He isn't ever late.
commonMistakes.why: 'Isn't' and 'never' are both negative, creating an unintended double negative - choose either the positive 'is' with 'never', or 'isn't' with 'ever'. This is the same double-negative pattern covered in an earlier lesson, appearing again in a slightly different form.

commonMistakes.right: I don't want any trouble.
commonMistakes.why: As with other present simple negatives, this needs 'don't' before the base verb, not 'not' placed directly in front of it. Keep building this pattern until it's fully automatic: subject + don't/doesn't + base verb.

commonMistakes.right: We aren't going there tomorrow. / We are not going there tomorrow.
commonMistakes.why: Since this describes a planned future action using present continuous, the negative needs 'are not/aren't', not just 'not' alone. Check which tense you're using first, then apply the matching negative structure (be + not, or do/does/did + not).

commonMistakes.wrong: I don't have to went there.
commonMistakes.right: I don't have to go there.
commonMistakes.why: After 'don't have to' (meaning something isn't necessary), the main verb must stay in its base form ('go'), not the past tense. Remember: 'have to' and its negative 'don't have to' are always followed by the base verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: She won't goes to the party.
commonMistakes.right: She won't go to the party.
commonMistakes.why: After 'won't' (will not), the main verb must stay in base form ('go'), not take an 's' ending. This follows the same rule as all modal verbs: modal + base verb, with no extra endings.

commonMistakes.right: I shouldn't have gone there alone. / I shouldn't go there alone.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence mixes up tenses - if you're talking about a past mistake, use 'shouldn't have gone' (regret about the past); if talking about future advice, use 'shouldn't go' (simple base verb). Choose the structure based on whether you're regretting a past action or advising about a future one.

commonMistakes.right: He didn't use to smoke.
commonMistakes.why: After 'didn't', the verb 'use to' loses its 'd' ending, since 'didn't' already carries the past tense meaning. This is a subtle spelling rule: didn't + use to (no d), not didn't + used to.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not understanding this lesson.
commonMistakes.right: I don't understand this lesson.
commonMistakes.why: 'Understand' is a state verb and isn't typically used in continuous form, even in the negative - use simple present negative instead. Stick with 'don't/doesn't understand' for state verbs like this one.

commonMistakes.right: They weren't at home.
commonMistakes.why: With 'be' verbs in the past tense, the negative is formed by adding 'not' directly after the verb (were + not = weren't), not by placing 'not' before the subject or verb separately. Keep the correct order: subject + wasn't/weren't + rest of sentence.

commonMistakes.right: I wasn't ready.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, the negative of 'was' is formed by adding 'not' right after it (wasn't), not by placing 'not' elsewhere in the sentence. Always attach 'not' directly to the be-verb it's negating.

commonMistakes.right: We haven't finished yet.
commonMistakes.why: This present perfect negative needs 'haven't' (have + not), with the correct word order, not 'not have' reversed. Keep repeating this pattern until it becomes automatic: subject + haven't/hasn't + past participle.

commonMistakes.wrong: I hope you not worry about this.
commonMistakes.right: I hope you don't worry about this.
commonMistakes.why: This present simple negative needs 'don't' before the verb, not 'not' placed directly before it without a helping verb. Always insert 'don't/doesn't' for present simple negatives, even in more complex sentences like this one.

commonMistakes.right: It doesn't matter to me.
commonMistakes.why: Once 'doesn't' is in place, the main verb must return to its base form ('matter'), not keep the 's' ending ('matters'). Remember: doesn't already includes the 's' meaning, so the verb itself stays plain.

commonMistakes.wrong: I no longer I like this song.
commonMistakes.right: I no longer like this song.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence accidentally repeats the subject 'I' - 'no longer' already modifies the verb directly, without needing the subject restated. Remove the second 'I' since the sentence only needs one subject.

commonMistakes.wrong: Not anyone came to the party.
commonMistakes.right: No one came to the party. / Not anyone came to the party (less common but acceptable).
commonMistakes.why: While technically both forms can work, 'no one' is the much more natural, standard way to express this idea in English. Prefer 'no one' or 'nobody' as your default choice for this kind of negative statement.

commonMistakes.wrong: I ain't got no time for this.
commonMistakes.right: I don't have any time for this. / I have no time for this.
commonMistakes.why: 'Ain't' is a non-standard, informal contraction not used in formal writing, and combined with 'no' here, it also creates a double negative. For standard English, use 'don't have any' or 'have no', avoiding 'ain't' entirely in formal contexts.

commonMistakes.wrong: I can't hardly wait for tomorrow.
commonMistakes.right: I can hardly wait for tomorrow.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'hardly' already carries a negative-like meaning (meaning 'barely'), combining it with 'can't' creates an unintended double negative. Use 'hardly' with a positive verb (can, will, is), never with a negative one.

commonMistakes.wrong: There is not many people here.
commonMistakes.right: There aren't many people here. / There are not many people here.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'people' is plural, the negative needs 'aren't' (are + not), matching the plural subject, not the singular 'is not'. Check subject-verb agreement in negative sentences just as carefully as in positive ones.

commonMistakes.wrong: I don't think this is not correct.
commonMistakes.right: I don't think this is correct. / I think this is not correct.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has an unnecessary double negative structure - having 'don't think' and 'not correct' together creates confusing, potentially contradictory meaning. Choose one negative: either negate the thinking ('don't think... is correct') or negate the fact ('think... is not correct'), not both.

commonMistakes.wrong: I couldn't never understand this topic.
commonMistakes.right: I could never understand this topic. / I couldn't ever understand this topic.
commonMistakes.why: 'Couldn't' and 'never' are both negative, creating a double negative - choose either the positive 'could' with 'never', or 'couldn't' with 'ever'. This double-negative pattern with modal verbs follows the same rule as with other negative words.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not certain not if this works.
commonMistakes.right: I am not certain if this works.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has an accidental double 'not' from careless writing - remove the extra one. Proofreading carefully for accidentally repeated negative words prevents this kind of mistake.

commonMistakes.wrong: He didn't wanted anything to eat.
commonMistakes.right: He didn't want anything to eat.
commonMistakes.why: As covered before, 'didn't' requires the base verb form afterward ('want'), never the past tense form ('wanted'). This mistake pattern is worth memorizing thoroughly since it appears so frequently.

commonMistakes.wrong: She isn't like her sister at all.
commonMistakes.right: She isn't like her sister at all. / She is not like her sister at all.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence is actually correct - 'isn't like' correctly uses 'like' as a preposition meaning 'similar to', which is different from the verb 'like' meaning 'enjoy'. This shows that 'like' can function as different parts of speech depending on context.

commonMistakes.right: I don't know anything anymore.
commonMistakes.why: This is the classic double negative pattern - 'don't' and 'nothing' together cancel out logically, so use 'anything' after the negative verb instead. This exact mistake type has appeared in earlier lessons too, showing how common and important it is to master.

commonMistakes.right: We aren't leaving yet.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has an unnecessary extra 'not' since 'aren't' already makes the sentence negative - adding 'not' again is a duplicate negative. Keep only one negative marker per clause; here, 'aren't' alone is sufficient.

commonMistakes.wrong: I don't want to not go.
commonMistakes.right: I don't want to go. / I want to not go, but I have no choice (rare, specific meaning).
commonMistakes.why: In almost all everyday contexts, simply saying 'I don't want to go' correctly expresses not wanting to attend - stacking 'don't want' with an additional 'not' before 'go' creates confusing, doubled negation. Stick with the simpler, single-negative version unless you have a very specific reason to emphasize refusal to not do something.

commonMistakes.wrong: They weren't never on time for meetings.
commonMistakes.right: They were never on time for meetings. / They weren't ever on time for meetings.
commonMistakes.why: 'Weren't' and 'never' are both negative, forming a double negative - choose either the positive 'were' with 'never', or 'weren't' with 'ever'. This is the same double-negative rule appearing once more with the past tense of 'be'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I haven't no idea what happened.
commonMistakes.right: I have no idea what happened. / I haven't any idea what happened.
commonMistakes.why: 'Haven't' and 'no' together create a double negative - choose either the positive 'have' with 'no', or the negative 'haven't' with 'any'. This common phrase is worth practicing correctly since it appears so often in daily conversation.

commonMistakes.right: It doesn't matter to me.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'it' is singular (like he/she), it needs 'doesn't', not 'don't', which is reserved for I/you/we/they. This is one of the most common subject-verb agreement mistakes in negative sentences, especially in casual speech.

commonMistakes.right: I no longer smoke. / I don't smoke anymore.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence combines two ways of expressing the same negative idea, creating an accidental double negative - choose either 'no longer' with a positive verb, or 'don't...anymore' with a negative verb. Pick one clean structure to express that a habit has stopped.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: Present simple negatives need 'don't/doesn't' plus the base verb.
After 'didn't', the verb must return to its base form, not stay in past tense.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: Modal verbs form their own negative directly (can't), with no 'to' or 'don't' added.

quiz.question: She ___ (not/have) time today. (doesn't + base verb)
quiz.explanation: With he/she/it, use 'doesn't' plus the base verb form.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: I don't have never been there.
I not have ever been there.
quiz.explanation: 'Never' already makes the sentence negative, so 'don't' shouldn't be added as well.
